coredata.dc:description: The significant production of oily industrial wastewater, fueled by industrial expansion, presents a major environmental risk and seriously threatens human health and the ecosystem. Traditional oily wastewater treatment processes suffer from drawbacks such as high energy consumption, low efficiency, and large footprint. This research aims to improve the efficiency of separation and antifouling properties of cellulose acetate (CA) tubular membranes for oil/water separation by incorporating tertiary amine carbon quantum dots (TQDs) into the membrane. The CA tubular membrane was fabricated using the dry-wet spinning technique. The TQDs increased the hydrophilicity of the CA membrane due to the presence of hydrophilic functional groups in the nanomaterial. The modified tubular membrane exhibited high permeance for pure water and diesel oil-water emulsion, measuring 586.8 ± 30.0 L m−2 h−1 bar−1 and 394.8 ± 43.3 L m−2 h−1 bar−1, respectively, with an impressive oil rejection rate of 99 %. The flux recovery and reversible fouling ratio increased to 89.81 % and 28.30 %, respectively, while the irreversible fouling ratio decreased to 10.19 %. The modified tubular membrane has a good long-term stability after 10 cycles of tests and effectively separated various oil (dodecane, diesel, canola oil, and engine oil)-water emulsions with a separation efficiency ranging from 97 % to 99 %. Overall, the tertiary amine carbon quantum dots-incorporated tubular membrane proved to be efficient in the separation of oil and water.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The significant production of oily industrial wastewater, fueled by industrial expansion, presents a major environmental risk and seriously threatens human health and the ecosystem. Traditional oily wastewater treatment processes suffer from drawbacks such as high energy consumption, low efficiency, and large footprint. This research aims to improve the efficiency of separation and antifouling properties of cellulose acetate (CA) tubular membranes for oil/water separation by incorporating tertiary amine carbon quantum dots (TQDs) into the membrane. The CA tubular membrane was fabricated using the dry-wet spinning technique. The TQDs increased the hydrophilicity of the CA membrane due to the presence of hydrophilic functional groups in the nanomaterial. The modified tubular membrane exhibited high permeance for pure water and diesel oil-water emulsion, measuring 586.8 ± 30.0 L m−2 h−1 bar−1 and 394.8 ± 43.3 L m−2 h−1 bar−1, respectively, with an impressive oil rejection rate of 99 %. The flux recovery and reversible fouling ratio increased to 89.81 % and 28.30 %, respectively, while the irreversible fouling ratio decreased to 10.19 %. The modified tubular membrane has a good long-term stability after 10 cycles of tests and effectively separated various oil (dodecane, diesel, canola oil, and engine oil)-water emulsions with a separation efficiency ranging from 97 % to 99 %. Overall, the tertiary amine carbon quantum dots-incorporated tubular membrane proved to be efficient in the separation of oil and water.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Oil-water separation is an indispensable process in mechanical, oil and gas industries, and chemical manufacturing as these industries produce oily wastewater that leads to environmental pollution, economic losses, harm to aquatic resources, and health risks [1,2]. Traditional methods for oil-water separation, like gravity separation, flotation, coagulation, and flocculation are costly and inefficient, particularly for the treatment of emulsified oil that has droplets smaller than 20 μm [3–5]. Emulsified oil is challenging to treat due to the oil’s small size and stability in water [6]. Ultrafiltration (UF) is a promising alternative to conventional methods that is cost effective, easy to operate, environment friendly and offers excellent selectivity [7,8].

In ultrafiltration systems, polymeric membranes are commonly utilized in oily wastewater treatment due to their favorable properties, cost-effectiveness, and ease of production [9]. Cellulose acetate (CA), a biodegradable polymeric membrane, is particularly notable for its efficient transport capabilities, non-toxic nature, and water affinity [10,11]. CA membranes achieve high separation efficiency because of their unique structure and properties, which promote easy functionalization and selective filtration of water molecules while blocking oil molecules [12]. Additionally, CA membranes are more affordable than other materials, making them widely applicable for oily wastewater treatment [13]. However, CA flat-sheet membranes have limited mechanical durability and are susceptible to fouling [14].

The mechanical limitations of flat-sheet membranes can be overcome by transforming them into tubular membranes such as reinforced hollow fiber membranes (HFMs). Reinforced HFMs offer several advantages over traditional flat-sheet membranes, including superior mechanical properties, larger surface area, increased porosity, and higher packing density [15,16]. One type of reinforced HFMs that finds wide industrial application is the braid-reinforced HFM that is consist of a tubular braid substrate coated with a polymer solution or a porous layer on its exterior [17]. Braid-reinforced HFMs are stronger and more durable than flat-sheet membranes which make them more suitable for commercialization and industrial applications [18]. The use of braid-reinforced HFMs also results in thinner membrane layers which reduces filtration resistance and increases flow rate and separation efficiency [15].

CA membrane is still prone to fouling despite its hydrophilic nature. When used in oily wastewater treatment, oil droplets build up on the membrane surface leading to membrane instability, reduced flux, and increased energy consumption [19–21]. Recent studies have shown that incorporating hydrophilic carbon-based nanomaterials can effectively mitigate membrane fouling [22–25]. Carbon quantum dots (CQDs), a type of carbon-based nanomaterial, have gained considerable attention because of their potential for various applications such as membrane additives to enhance the hydrophilicity and antifouling of the membrane for oil-water separation [26,27]. Unlike traditional quantum dots, CQDs offer several advantages including excellent optical properties, hydrophilicity, low toxicity, strong biocompatibility, ease of functionalization, high water solubility, and cost-effectiveness [28,29]. The hydrophilic nature of CQDs is due to the presence of multiple functional groups, such as hydroxyl (–OH) and carboxyl (–COOH) groups, which do not only provide excellent water solubility but also enable versatile functionalization [30,31].

The primary aim of this study was to enhance the efficiency of CA tubular membranes for oil/water separation by incorporating tertiary amine carbon quantum dots (TQDs) into the membrane. The TQD nanoparticles were synthesized by reacting CQDs with N, N′-diisopropylcarbodiimide (DIC) and N-hydroxysuccinimide (NHS) followed by the amination reaction of three tertiary amine monomers—4-amino-1-methylpiperidine (4AMP), N-N-dimethyl-1,3-propanediamine (DMEDA), and N-N-dimethyl-1,3-propanediamine (DMPDA)— of different carbon chain length and structure. These newly formed TQDs nanoparticles were incorporated into CA tubular membranes to achieve increased hydrophilicity and oleophobicity, excellent separation efficiency, and enhanced antifouling properties. The pristine and modified CA tubular membranes were fabricated using the dry-wet spinning method, and their permeation, long-term stability, and physical, chemical, and antifouling properties were subsequently assessed.

Cellulose acetate (CA) powder (CA-394–60S) was acquired from Eastman Chemical Company, CA, USA. Reagent grade N-methyl-2-pyrrolidone (NMP) and dodecane (95 %) were obtained from Tedia Company, Inc. (OH, United States). Polyester tubular supporter (diameter = 1.8 mm) was procured from King Membrane Energy Technology Inc. (Tainan, Taiwan). Citric acid (anhydrous, 99.5 %), sodium hydroxide (NaOH), and sodium dodecyl sulfate (SDS, 99.5 % purity) were supplied by Showa Chemical Co. Ltd. (Tokyo, Japan). N-hydroxysuccinimide (NHS) was obtained from Alfa Aesar Co. N, N′-diisopropylcarbodiimide (DIC, 98 %), and 4-amino-1-methylpiperidine (4AMP, 97.5 %) was procured from Macklin Inc. (Shanghai, China). N-N-dimethyl-1,3-propanediamine (DMPDA, 99 %) and N, N -dimethylethylenediamine (DMEDA, 99 %) were acquired from Thermo Fisher Scientific Inc. (Leicestershire, United Kingdom). Hydrochloric acid (HCl, analytical grade) was procured from Aencore Chemical Pty., Ltd. (Melbourne, Australia). Ethanol (95 %) was obtained from Echo Chemical Co. Ltd. (Tainan, Taiwan). Canola oil, diesel oil, and engine oil were purchased from Weiyi Enterprises Company Ltd. (Kaohsiung, Taiwan), CPC Corporation (Kaohsiung, Taiwan), and Exxon Mobil Corporation (TX, United States), respectively.

The CQDs were synthesized according to the method described in our previous work [32]. Briefly, 4 g of dehydrated citric acid was placed in a hydrothermal autoclave and heated at 210°C for 3 hours. The citric acid, initially in a solid white form, was condensed into an orange paste, signifying the successful formation of CQDs. Next, 500 g of distilled water, 4 g of DIC, and 3.2 g of NHS were added to the CQDs solution, and the resulting mixture was stirred for 4 hours at room temperature. The by-product 1,3- diisopropylurea (1,3-DIU) was filtered using filter paper. The filtrate containing the CQDs was then added with 8 g of tertiary amine monomer (4AMP, DMPDA or DMEDA) and stirred at room temperature for 45 min. After which, the solution was neutralized to pH = 7 using 0.1 M HCl followed by a 6-hour dialysis process utilizing a 1000-Dalton (Da) dialysis tube with water being replaced every hour. Lastly, the solution was evaporated in a rotary evaporator at a temperature of 70°C and the concentrate was placed in a freeze dryer for 24 h. Three distinct powders were obtained, namely TQDs-A (4AMP), TQDs-B (DMPDA), and TQDs-C (DMEDA). The proposed amidation mechanism of CQDs is shown in Fig. 1 [33].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of pristine CA and QDs/CA membrane
ce:section.ce:para: The pristine CA and QDs/CA tubular membranes were prepared separately through the dry-wet spinning method as illustrated in Figure S2. The pristine CA membrane was prepared by adding CA powder into the NMP solution at a fixed concentration of 15 wt%. For the QDs-incorporated membrane, quantum dots were dispersed in NMP using a homogenizer at 12,000 rpm for 30 min. Then, CA powder was added to the QDs-NMP solution at 15 wt%. The dope solution was completely dissolved at 90 °C for 8 hours. After achieving complete dissolution, the CA/NMP and QDs/CA/NMP solution was degassed overnight. This polymer solution was then pumped through a spinneret, where it was extruded onto the outer surface of a tubular PET substrate (Figure S3). Then, it was immediately immersed in a water bath (coagulation tank 1) maintained at 25 ± 2 °C. An air gap of 15 cm was maintained between the spinneret and the coagulation bath. Following this initial coagulation, the tubular membrane was collected by a take-up wheel and submerged in a second water bath for further solvent removal. The fabrication of the tubular membranes involved a take-up speed and membrane thickness of 1.0 m/min and 225 µm, respectively. The pristine CA and QDs/CA tubular membranes were washed multiple times with deionized (DI) water. Then, the membranes were kept in DI water for subsequent performance testing and characterization. The components of the polymer dope solutions are provided in Table 1.

The chemical and elemental compositions of membranes and quantum dots were assessed using attenuated total reflectance–Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (ATR-FTIR, Spectrum One FTIR Spectrometer, PerkinElmer, MA, USA), and X-ray photoelectron spectrometry (XPS, VG K-Alpha, Thermo Fisher Scientific Inc., United Kingdom). Morphological analysis was conducted using field emission scanning electron microscopy with energy dispersive X-ray (FESEM-EDX, Model S-4800, Hitachi Co., Tokyo, Japan) and atomic force microscopy (AFM, NanoScope V, Bruker Scientific Instruments, MA, USA). ImageJ software was utilized to measure the pore size and surface porosity. The hydrophilicity and oleophobicity of the membranes were assessed using an interfacial tensiometer (PD-VP Type, FACE Automatic Interfacial Tensiometer, Kyowa Interface Science Co. Ltd., Saitama, Japan).

The same laboratory crossflow filtration setup and tubular membrane module as in our previous study [33] were used for measuring the permeability and antifouling properties. First, an oil-water emulsion was prepared by blending oil (diesel, dodecane, canola or engine) with water. SDS was then added into the emulsion and stirred at 1500 rpm for 8 h. The ratio of oil and SDS to water was maintained at 1:99 (w/w). Subsequently, the tubular membrane was introduced into the crossflow filtration setup. Then, DI water was fed in the crossflow setup at 0.25 bar with a flow rate of 1 L/min. The steady-state condition was achieved after 30 min. The permeance (F, L m−2 h−1 bar−1, LMHB) and oil rejection rate (%R) were calculated using Eqs. (1) and (2):

Each cycle of antifouling test using the CA tubular membrane consisted of a 60-min pure water permeance (PWP) test followed by a 60-min permeance measurement. Deionized (DI) water was first fed into the crossflow filtration setup at 0.25 bar and a flow rate of 1 L/min for 30 min until steady-state condition was achieved; then, the PWP was measured at 10-min intervals for 1 h. Subsequently, the feed was changed to oil-water emulsion, and the permeance was measured at 10-min intervals for 1 h. After each cycle, the membrane module was extracted, and the system was thoroughly washed with 1000 ppm SDS for 10 min and rinsed with DI water for another 10 min. The membrane was then reinserted into the setup and washed with DI water for 10 min. This procedure was repeated for three and a half cycles of PWP, oil-water separation, and washing to evaluate the antifouling properties. The flux recovery ratio (FRR, %), total fouling ratio (Rt, %), reversible fouling ratio (Rr, %), and irreversible fouling ratio (Rir, %) were then calculated using Eqs. (3–6):

The stability of the pristine CA and optimal (TQDs-C/CA) membranes was examined by performing a long-term UF performance test. Each membrane was first immersed in DI water for 3 days before conducting the ultrafiltration performance test using diesel oil-water emulsion repeatedly for 10 consecutive cycles.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Structural and chemical analysis of the quantum dots
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 2 shows the TEM images of the synthesized CQDs and TQDs. Typically, CQDs manifest as quasi-spherical nanoparticles with diameters varying from 3–20 nm. The mean diameters of the CQDs, TQDs-A, TQDs-B, and TQDs-C were 2.7 ± 1.1, 3.4 ± 1.1, 3.8 ± 0.8 and 4.1 ± 0.4 nm, respectively. The structure and the size of the modified TQDs were relatively similar to those of the CQDs [34]. Furthermore, the AFM images and height profiles of the CQDs and TQDs are shown in Figure S4. It can be seen that there are small round structures which confirms the quasi-spherical shape of QDs. However, the height of the CQDs corresponds roughly to that of a single quantum dot while their width was significantly larger. This discrepancy could be attributed to an AFM imaging known as "tip imaging." Alternatively, it is possible that these small structures are not individual quantum dots but rather cluster of quantum dots attached together [35]. All the QDs are less than 10 nm in height which is consistent with the TEM images. The images of the aqueous QDs solution under ultraviolet (UV) irradiation light are shown in Figure S5. When the solution was subjected to UV light, it emitted a bright blue color, indicating a strong quantum confinement effect, which signifies successful synthesis of the nanomaterials [36,37]. It is to be noted that the color of quantum dots under UV light depends on the particle size and QDs with a particle size smaller than 10 nm emit a bright blue color [37].

The ATR-FTIR spectra of the QDs are shown in Fig. 3a. The synthesized CQDs exhibited the following peaks: C-O vibrations at 1078 cm−1; C-O-C vibrations at 1278 cm−1; C-OH vibrations at 1385 cm−1; OCO vibrations at 1550 cm−1; CO stretch at 1650 cm−1; OH stretch at 3320 cm−1 [38]. New characteristic peaks were observed with the TQDs at 2948 and 1641 cm−1, 2948 and 1641 cm−1, and 2944 and 1652 cm−1 for TQDS-A, TQDs-B, and TQDs-C, respectively which correspond to CH2 [39] and CO [40] stretching. Furthermore, peaks were found at 1460, 1465, and 1462 cm−1 for TQDs-A, TQDs-B, and TQDs-C, respectively, corresponding to CH2 scissoring [41]. There were also new characteristics peaks at 1145 and 830 cm−1, 1148 and 830 cm−1, and 1131 and 835 cm−1 for TQDs-A, TQDs-B, and TQDs-C, respectively, which could be attributed to C-N-C [42] and C-N [43] bonds.

Fig. 3b and Table S1 display the respective XPS survey spectra and elemental compositions of the QDs. As a result of the reaction between the tertiary amine monomers and the CQDs, the nitrogen concentration in the TQDs increased while the oxygen concentration decreased. Furthermore, TQDs-A and TQDs-B contained more carbon content compared to TQDs-C because TQDs-A had a carbon ring and TQDs-B had a longer carbon chain. The TQDs survey spectra revealed a new nitrogen peak, which confirmed the successful synthesis of the TQDs. In Fig. 3c, the C1s narrow scan of CQDs showed the C-C, C-O, and CO peaks at 284.1, 285.7, and 287.5 eV, respectively. Furthermore, peaks at ∼399 and ∼401 eV were observed in the N1s narrow scan of the TQDs (Fig. 3d-f), which were associated with C-N and N-H-CO bonds, respectively [44–46]. This result further confirmed the synthesis of the TQDs from the reaction of the tertiary amine monomers and CQDs.

The morphologies of the pristine CA and QDs/CA membranes are displayed in Fig. 4. As shown in Fig. 4a1-e1, the QD-containing membranes were more porous compared with the pristine CA membrane which could be attributed to the presence of the hydrophilic functional groups such as carboxyl and amine groups in the quantum dots. As shown in Table 2, the pore size increased slightly from 11.25 ± 0.96 (CA) to 13.67 ± 0.58 nm (TQDs-C/CA) and surface porosity increased slightly from 0.43 ± 0.18 (CA) to 1.01 ± 0.10 % (TQDs-C/CA). When hydrophilic nanoparticles such as tertiary amine quantum dots were added to the polymer solution, it led to an increase in the non-solvent inflow rate during membrane formation, which resulted in the development of a porous membrane surface [47,48].

The measured skin layer thickness of the membranes is presented in Fig. 4 a2-d2 and Table 2. The data followed the opposite trend observed with the pore size and surface porosity of the membranes. This can be attributed to the presence of hydrophilic nanomaterial in the casting solution which caused an immediate separation of the non-solvent and solvent during membrane formation resulting in a thinner skin layer. The AFM images and the surface roughness parameters are shown in Fig. 4 a3-d3 and Table 2, respectively. It can be noted that the surface roughness was consistent with the trend for the pore size and surface porosity. This is to be expected as membranes that exhibit high surface roughness correspond to a more porous surface [49]. The increase in surface roughness allows water molecules to come into contact with a larger surface area, promoting water flow through the membrane; hence, improving the permeance.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Chemical analysis and wetting properties of membranes
ce:section.ce:para: The FTIR spectra of pristine CA and QDs/CA tubular membranes are shown in Fig. 5a. The stretching vibration of the OH group in CA was identified at 3500 cm−1. Distinct peaks corresponding to the stretching of CH and the vibrations of CO and CH groups appeared at 2945 cm−1, 1743 cm−1, and 1372 cm−1, respectively [50,51]. Additionally, vibration peaks at 1230 cm−1 and 1044 cm−1 indicated the symmetric -ROR group of cellulosic esters [52]. The spectra of the modified membrane did not show any apparent change in the characteristic peaks due to the low concentrations of quantum dots incorporated in the membrane resulting in insufficient intensity of the characteristic peak signal in FTIR. Furthermore, the characteristic peak of CA overlapped with some of the peaks of CQDs/CA and TQDs/CA tubular membranes. Lastly, the ATR-FTIR could detect only within 1 µm of the membrane’s active layer [53]. Therefore, additional EDX and XPS analyses were performed to confirm the presence of quantum dots in the CA membrane.

The EDX mapping shows the distribution of carbon, oxygen, and nitrogen on the cross-section ( Fig. 6) and surface (Figure S6) and of the membranes. The EDX images indicated that carbon and oxygen were uniformly distributed throughout the surface and cross-section of the tubular membranes due to the presence of hydroxyl and carbonyl groups in the CA membrane. In the CA and CQDs/CA, no nitrogen was detected because of the absence of amino groups in the membrane. The EDX images of all TQDs/CA showed the presence N element from the tertiary amine of the TQDs. The XPS survey and elemental composition of CA tubular membranes are shown in Table S2 and Fig. 5b, respectively. The results supported the data obtained from the EDX analysis; no detectable nitrogen was observed in the pristine and CQDs/CA membranes. The ratio of carbon to oxygen in the CQDs/CA was altered due to the incorporation of CQDs into the CA membrane. There was an increase in oxygen due to the carboxyl groups of the CQDs. All the TQDs/CA membranes increased in nitrogen content, which could be attributed to the tertiary amine of the TQDs. The presence of CQDs and TQDs within the QDs/CA tubular membranes was therefore confirmed by the results obtained from XPS and EDX analyses.

Fig. 7a shows the water contact angle (WCA) of the pristine CA and QDs/CA membranes. The WCA of the pristine CA membrane was 60.5 ± 1.2°. The pristine CA membrane was generally hydrophilic due to the presence of carbonyl and hydroxyl functional groups. The WCA of the CQDs/CA and all TQDs/CA were lower than that of the pristine CA membrane. The incorporation of quantum dots in the membrane decreased the WCA because of the hydrophilic functional group of the CQDs and TQDs, such as the carboxyl and amino groups. The TQDs-C/CA had the lowest water contact angle (56.6 ± 0.9°) because of the shorter hydrocarbon chain structure of the DMEDA monomer. Generally, shorter hydrocarbon chains are more hydrophilic compared to longer hydrocarbon chains [54]. The underwater oil contact angle (UWOCA) of the pristine CA and QDs/CA membranes are presented in Fig. 7b. The observed trend in the diesel oil contact angle was the opposite of that observed with the WCA. The UWOCA of the CA pristine membrane (125.9 ± 0.6°) was lower than that of CQDs/CA (127.1 ± 0.5°), TQDs-A/CA (130.8 ± 0.5°), TQDs-B/CA (131.3 ± 0.6°), and TQDs-C/CA (131.9 ± 0.7°) with TQDs-C/CA being the most oleophobic. These results imply that diesel oil has less contact with the membrane surface, resulting in a higher oil contact angle. These data suggest that incorporating quantum dots into the CA membrane improved the membrane's oleophobicity.

The PWP and permeance for diesel-water separation of the pristine CA and QDs/CA tubular membranes are presented in Fig. 8a and b. The pristine CA tubular membrane had a PWP of 530.1 ± 20.4 L m−2 h−1 bar−1. Among the modified membranes, the TQDs-C/CA tubular membrane exhibited the highest PWP (586.8 ± 30.0 L m−2 h−1 bar−1). Also, the TQDs-C tubular membrane showed the highest permeance (394.8 ± 43.3 L m−2 h−1 bar−1) for diesel-water separation. All tubular membranes demonstrated an oil rejection rate of > 98 %, indicating their excellent efficiency in oil-water separation processes. TQD-C/CA had the highest oil rejection of 99.2 ± 0.6 %. The efficient separation was mainly caused by the enhanced hydrophilicity and oleophobicity of the membrane due to the incorporated quantum dots. It should be noted a membrane has good oleophobic property if the WCA is less than 90° and the UWOCA is more than 90° [55]. The incorporation of CQDs and TQDs increased the hydrophilic functional groups such as carboxyl and amine present in the CA membrane; thereby, enhancing its water affinity and oleophobicity. Furthermore, the modified QDs/CA membranes had larger pore sizes, higher surface porosity, rougher surface, and thinner skin layer that facilitated greater water molecule passage and increased PWP and permeance for oil-water separation. Moreover, TQDs-C/CA has the highest PWP and permeance because it contained the shortest hydrocarbon chain structure of DMEDA monomer compared with the other monomers. The other monomers either contained a cyclic ring (4AMP) or much longer carbon chain (DMPDA) which makes it slightly hydrophobic compared to DMEDA.

Fig. 9a shows the antifouling test results for the pristine CA and TQDs-C/CA tubular membranes. The TQDs-C/CA membrane exhibited higher water permeance compared to the pristine CA membrane after 3.5 cycles of antifouling tests. This improved performance was due to the increased hydrophilicity and oleophobicity of TQDs-C/CA, which reduced oil molecule blockage, allowing more water to pass through. These observations were consistent with the antifouling properties (flux recovery ratio, reversible fouling ratio, and irreversible fouling ratio) of the membranes presented in Fig. 9b and Table 3. The addition of TQDs-C enhanced the flux recovery and minimized irreversible fouling of the membrane. This is due to the carboxyl and amine functional group that attracted the water molecules on the membrane surface, which acted as a fouling barrier [46]. TQDs-C/CA had the highest flux recovery (89.81 %) and reversible fouling (28.30 %), and the lowest irreversible fouling (10.19 %) because of the increased hydrophilicity, which prevented the retention of oil molecules on the membrane surface, facilitating easier removal of fouling contaminants with simple washing. Fig. 9c and Table 3 illustrate the FRR data during the 3.5 testing cycles. The FRR of the TQDs-C/CA membrane consistently remained at 75.16 % after the test, which is significantly higher than the 61.11 % FRR of the pristine CA membrane. This substantial improvement is attributed to the enhanced hydrophilicity and antifouling properties of the TQDs-C/CA membrane due to the incorporation of TQDs-C.

Fig. 9d shows the long-term stability of TQDs-C/CA membrane for use in diesel-oil water emulsion separation. After the first cycle (60 min) of separation, the permeance of CA decreased from 100 % to 90.0 %. while the TQDs-C/CA only decreased to 94.7 %. After washing, the CA only increased to 90.4 % while the TQDs-C/CA increased up to 97.9 %. At the last cycle of separation, CA permeance can only maintained to 57.35 % while TQDs-C/CA maintained to 81.36 % of flux. Furthermore, the oil rejection (Fig. 9e) using TQDs-C/CA was maintained at 99.0 % for 10 cycles. These results suggest that the TQDs-C/CA membrane could undergo repeated cleaning without losing its hydrophilicity and excellent oleophobic property.

Fig. 10a shows the performance of TQDs-C/CA for the separation of different oil-water emulsions. Various oils— dodecane, diesel, canola oil, and engine oil— were used to evaluate the performance of the tubular membrane in oil-water separation. The TQDs-C/CA tubular membrane achieved a rejection rate exceeding 97 % for all oil samples, demonstrating its exceptional efficiency in the separation of different oil-water emulsions. The size distribution of oil droplets in both the feed and permeate measured by DLS are shown in Fig. 10b and c. The highest rejection rate (99.79 ± 0.01 %) was obtained with engine oil, which could be attributed to its larger droplet size. It was followed by canola oil (99.66 ± 0.03 %), diesel oil (99.25 ± 0.60 %), and dodecane (97.47 ± 1.56 %). The observed trend corresponded to the size distribution of the different oil-water emulsions as larger oil droplets are less likely to penetrate the membrane's pores. On the contrary, the lowest rejection rate was observed with dodecane because it has a smaller oil droplet size that can easily pass through the membrane pores. From the size distribution of the permeate and feed solution, the curve of the permeate shows a single peak around 50–100 nm, indicating that tiny oil droplets can still pass through the membrane and micelles of surfactant were formed through the aggregations of SDS in the oil-water emulsions [56–58].

Fig. 11 presents optical microscopy and actual images of the feed and permeate. As shown, the feed contained visible oil droplets, whereas the permeate had tiny oil droplets in an almost clear solution. These findings suggest that most of the oil droplets were removed during the crossflow ultrafiltration. Overall, this demonstrates that the TQDs-C/CA tubular membrane has a high efficiency in rejecting different types of oil-water emulsions. Additionally, the actual sample images of the feed (left) and permeate (right) for various oil-water emulsion separation tests using TQDs-C/CA tubular membranes show a significant difference between the feed, which appeared milky white and the transparent permeate could be observed.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Comparison of the TQDs/CA membrane performance for oil-water separation with existing literature
ce:section.ce:para: Table 4 compares the TQDs-C/CA membranes with other polymeric membranes used for the separation of oil-water emulsion. The results demonstrate that the TQDs-C/CA membrane exhibits excellent performance, as considered by its high pure water permeance, emulsion permeance, and oil rejection rate. These findings suggest that the TQDs-modified tubular membrane has significant potential for efficient and practical application in oil-water emulsion separation processes.

Pristine CA and different tertiary amine carbon quantum dots (TQDs)/CA tubular membranes were fabricated using the dry-wet spinning method. TQDs were synthesized by reacting CQDs with DIC and NHS, followed by the reaction of three different tertiary amine monomers (4AMP, DMEDA, or DMPDA). The incorporation of TQDs into the CA membrane improved the morphology and wetting properties of the membranes due to the presence of hydrophilic functional groups in the nanomaterial. Among the three different TQDs/CA, the TQDs-C/CA tubular membrane exhibited superior ultrafiltration performance, with PWP of 586.8 ± 30.0 L m−2 h−1 bar−1, permeance of 394.8 ± 43.3 L m−2 h−1 bar−1, and a separation efficiency > 99 %. It also demonstrated good antifouling properties (flux recovery ratio = 89.81 %, reversible fouling ratio = 28.30 %, irreversible fouling ratio = 10.19 %). Furthermore, the TQDs-C/CA tubular membrane achieved a high rejection rate (97–99 %) for various oil (dodecane, diesel, canola, and engine oil)-water emulsions) and has good long-term stability after 10 cycles of test. In conclusion, the TQDs-C/CA tubular membranes can effectively separate oil in water emulsions.
